739000.reason: This server manages HVAC equipment in several buildings throughout campus including labs, the library and other academic buildings. The buildings cannot be converted to another system without a large capitol investment as it requires replacing much of the HVAC equipment itself. The OS is end of life and will not receive updates. Excessive network traffic has caused the server to crash in the past and required a hard reboot. I believe that we already had an exception in place for this system but that was before we had a process for proper exception requests. It is likely unsupported by either the EDR nor vulnerability management agent software.

Jennifer

Request Tracker is our ticketing system and uses email to receive/create tickets. Historically, this has been used by several groups within DAR and has been used with non-UD people. So until we can confirm there is no longer a need to receive email from any external email server, we need to keep this open for receiving emails on port 25.

816094.reason:
The college of engineering has a mail server that is used to automatically generate mailing lists for CoE departments (Faculty, Staff, Researchers, Postdocs, Grad Students, Undergrad Students [Freshmen, Sophomore, Juniors, Seniors], etc) and has rules to automatically include/exclude specific individuals as needed. This system is firewalled off by CoE-IT to only allow specific mail servers to reach it. Emails are actually first send to a UD Google Group (to provide @udel.edu addresses) and those get sent to the CoE mail server. Upon arrival, the mail system checks that the header includes the correct Google Group ID number and then double checks that the mail sender is valid before attempting to process any mail.

As such, we need to keep the stmp ports open on this machine to ensure that timely notifications to end users.

This box also has an HTTPS web GUI that is only used on campus (behind CAS), that is used to modify exceptions lists and authenticated senders.
816094.riskAssessment: This is for automating emailing lists to ensure no student, faculty, nor staff member is missed in communications as the lists are generated in real time from nightly data feeds.
816094.dataLevelStored: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
816094.dataAccessLevel: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
816094.mitigation: Much of this has been noted above. We do use a top of rack firewall to limit inbound smtp access to only Google, MS Exchange, Comcast (done for testing purposes) mail servers. Crowdstrike has been on the existing server for some time.

867270.reason:
The Special Collections and Museums Division of the Library, Museums and Press seeks to build its collections in support of the research and learning mission of the University of Delaware. These collections are open and available to faculty, students, and the public in support of scholarship and general interest. These collections have been developed over many years, acquired either through purchase or donation.  Historically these collections have consisted of physical artifacts, but the Special Collections Department has a growing born-digital preservation program that enables it to acquire born digital content, such as computer files and media. 

 

Born-digital items are materials that are created in a digital format. This includes websites, email, digital photographs, electronic records, and more.  This material can be acquired on various digital media including hard drive, floppy disc, compact disc, usb stick, and other removable media. 

 

Sometimes this media has been put together specifically to transport born-digital content, but other times represents working media that may provide access to deleted files and other forensic information.  For this reason it is important that this media not  be disturbed or altered in any way outside of the direct intervention by Special Collections staff in the course of their work to make the data available to users.  Regardless of whether the content is still on the media it arrived on or has been copied to a system for processing, no born-digital content should not be altered until examined.  Special tools are used to examine and process media and content, and physical write blocker devices are used to ensure media is not altered when accessed. 

 

There may also be restrictions on who can see or access specific born-digital content that may be limited either via a formal Non-Disclosure agreement or through clauses in the Deed of Gift, including most Library, Museums and Press staff.  Many of these collections involve political papers. 

 

The goal of these exception policies is to ensure that CrowdStrike does not alter or expose born-digital  content in any way.  To this end the associated prevention policies have detection fully enabled, but some prevention and learning options have disabled.
867270.hostnames:
eprocessing.lib.udel.edu (Linux, Ubuntu 22LTS, required software does not support (Ubuntu 24LTS)
(2) end user laptops  (not dns'ed)
867270.riskAssessment:
Please see "Reason for Request"

Details of differences to Phase 3 best practices detailed in attached document.
Windows policy set deviates in 6 settings based around quarantining and potential uploads to Crowdstrike servers
Linux policy does not deviate from phase 2 best practices.

It would have to be a fresh new OS install. As it is a 13 year old hardware, there is a question of support in new versions of Linux.  Since the system is very static with almost no user activity (only a handful of users have smb login access rights), this does not seem to be worth the effort to bring it up to date, hence the request to leave it alone and do not scan it.

997477.reason:
Several of our BMC/service processor interfaces have tripped the security scanners.

We interface with these mainly to provide an IPMI/server management interface to the servers at hand.  We already have these processors on the 10. private network, and would be willing to firewall them more strictly, e.g. each communication to each IP could be restricted to its own VLAN, or even a specific management host, TBD.
997477.riskAssessment: Patching/updating these endpoints is not feasible.  It would most not even be possible in some cases.  In other cases, it would require patching firmwares/BIOSes which is time consuming and error prone, bringing critical site resources down for too long (with a danger of indefinite downtime in the worst case).  I am also leaving the University and would not have time to patch these.
997477.dataLevelStored: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
997477.dataLevelStoredSpecified: Data with low confidentiality, integrity and availability concerns
997477.dataAccessLevel: Level I - Low impact: public information (eg., event schedules)
997477.dataAccessLevelSpecified: Data with low confidentiality, integrity and availability concerns
997477.impactedSystems: These are not full systems with firewalls, OSs, etc.  They are peripheral devices to provide access to existing servers, which *are* patched regularly and maintained behind firewalls, etc.
997477.mitigation: We use encrypted credentials to log into these peripheral endpoints, without which a user (theoretically) could gain access to these BMC endpoints.  Even access to these endpoints does not confer access to the systems, as the logins for those would still be needed on the remote console.  The worst an attacker could do, with unmitigated access to these endpoints, is to view the system console and/or reboot or shut down the parent system(s).

1019629.reason:
The Library, Museums and Press has a group of computers  that are secured, 'frozen' using the Faronics Deep Freeze.  These computers are in areas accessible to the public after normal business hours, areas that can be minimally or unmonitored at certain times when Morris Library is still open.  Freezing them protects against tampering or  undesired changes.   Unfortunately freezing them also hinders, regular active updates since they are vulnerable to change while unfrozen, such as  for patching purposes.  Library IT has established two patching windows for these systems in between Fall and Spring semester.  All systems are fully patched before Fall or Spring semester begins.

Installing Tenable on these systems would just generate alerts that could not/would not be dealt with for months.  Given that we request an exception to installing the Tenable agent on these frozen systems.

Attached is a list of the 198 frozen systems in Morris Library, including loaner systems.  The list includes system names and ip addresses.  Loaner and instructional laptops are  wifi configured only
1019629.riskAssessment: These systems are protected by Crowdstrike and revert to their original state on reboot.  While it is valuable  to know potentially vulnerabilities, it is useless if not acted upon in a timely  fashion, and being frozen precludes this.  There is a much greater risk of tampering and exploits if these computers were not frozen.
1019629.impactedSystems: Public,  loaner, and public service desk client systems.  Level 1 data only.  No firewall exceptions.  Low impact.  Non-critical.

1019927.reason:
The laptops will be used in a Pod (clean room) at our Blackhawk facility. The Pod does not have wired network access. At present, there are cost constraints that preclude adding network access to the Pods. The laptop will be connected via USB to process equipment and will run the equipment software and collect research data. The data will be transferred to USB drive for removal from the clean room for further analysis on NIIMBL managed equipment. The laptop will be setup as follows:
  • Auto login to desktop (non-admin account)
  • WiFi disabled in BIOS (they should not be connected to any network)
  • BitLocker enabled (keys stored in BitWarden)
  • No domain join, or any other NIIMBL/UD software.
In short, the laptop will be stand-alone and thus will not be domain joined or run any management or security agents.
1019927.riskAssessment: Laptops are essential for operating and interfacing with laboratory equipment in the Clean Room
1019927.dataLevelStored: Level II - Moderate impact: 'official use' information (eg., work emails, internal docs)
1019927.dataAccessLevel: Level II - Moderate impact: 'official use' information (eg., work emails, internal docs)
1019927.impactedSystems: The data will be transferred to USB drive for removal from the clean room for further analysis on NIIMBL managed equipment.
1019927.mitigation: The data will be transferred to USB drive for removal from the clean room for further analysis on NIIMBL managed equipment.

1024559.reason:
donbot.dbi.udel.edu -10.2.123.89 is a Vmware ESXi server. It hosts three VMs which are still in use: PaperCut server, FlexLM license manager, and a Win server instance.  Moving them to a different platform would be difficult, and very disruptive.  FlexLM licenses are tied up to the actual hardware. They cannot be migrated, they have to be reissued. Similar issue with PaperCut.  

Can't we leave this system alone?  It resides quitely on 10.2 subnet. It does not bother anyone. There are no user accounts on it. The risk is minimal, if any at all. 

Moving the above services to a different platform in in our long term - such as 12 months - plans. This will be either another virtualization platform, such as Proxmox, or individual physical servers.  But please just leave it alone for the time being.
1024559.riskAssessment:
Most of the reasons and impact have been described above. To repeat myself here: The server hosts three VMs which: PaperCut server, FlexLM license manager, and a Win server instance.  Moving them to a different platform would be difficult, and very disruptive.  FlexLM licenses are tied up to the actual hardware. They cannot be migrated, they have to be reissued. Similar issue with PaperCut.  We cannot afford any long term interruptions to these services.
The risk is minimal, if any at all. There are no user accounts on the server in question, just a couple of admin level accounts protected by secure passwords. Also, there are no higher level user data. There is really nothing of value there.
1024559.impactedSystems: These are non-critical no risk data. Absolutely nothing of value.

1049296.reason:
I have a digital signage project running and approved within the College of Arts and Sciences that's running Windows 11 ARM on Raspberry Pi 4, 8GB devices. I am using WoR (Windows on Raspberry) to boot in Win11 ARM, which then loads the Zoom Room digital signage only, Application.  This project ran smoothly for 2 1/2 years until a recent CrowdStrike update. I have reached out to CrowdStrike, UD IT and my leadership to resolve the issue. My leadership has approved my request to file an exception on these devices. I will have to rebuild them and will lock down and document all unnecessary firewall services and background services that I will have disabled. Only the Zoom Rooms app and some Windows services need to run on these devices. They also only currently work on Ethernet, so they will not be connected to any Wi-Fi services. 

I need this exception because any CAS managed Windows device is being managed by MECM, which is automatically pushing the CS sensor down to these Raspberry Pi devices. Again, these devices were working fine for years, until a recent CS sensor update. CS has confirmed with me that they do not support the processor on the RB Pi. I have already tried running CSuninstall and other methods of removing CrowdStrike, but because these devices now only boot into safe mode, I need to restart this process. 

Departments within CAS as waiting for their digital displays to be active again, so I am hoping this exception can be filed soon.

- CrowdStrike troubleshooting 
- Rebuild of the image
